title: Flux-Mediated Correspondence Between Real- and Momentum-Space Nonsymmorphicity
authors: Z. Y. Chen, Y. X. Zhao
abstract: Momentum-space nonsymmorphic symmetries have recently attracted significant interest in both artificial and condensed-matter crystals, whereas real-space nonsymmorphic symmetries have long played an important role in the study of crystalline topological phases. Here, we establish a general theory of momentum-space crystallographic groups that emerge from projective representations of real-space crystallographic groups in the presence of gauge flux, applicable in particular to real-space nonsymmorphic groups. A central result is a flux-mediated ``bi-nonsymmorphicity'' relation that reveals a structural correspondence between real-space and momentum-space nonsymmorphicity mediated by gauge flux. This relation implies that, under a symmetric gauge flux, real-space nonsymmorphicity can enforce momentum-space nonsymmorphicity, and that in some cases a symmetric gauge flux requires nonsymmorphicity in both real and momentum space. Our work not only identifies a fundamental structure in projective crystal symmetries, but also provides guiding principles for designing artificial crystals and condensed-matter platforms that exhibit both real-space and momentum-space nonsymmorphic symmetries.
summary: This paper establishes a fundamental theoretical link between real-space and momentum-space nonsymmorphic symmetries mediated by gauge flux. By using group cohomology, the authors prove that a specific flux configuration can force a crystal to exhibit nonsymmorphic features in the Brillouin zone even if they were not explicitly present in the real-space description. This provides a guiding principle for designing new topological materials and metamaterials with engineered momentum-space symmetries.
main_problem: The paper investigates the relationship between real-space and momentum-space nonsymmorphic symmetries in the presence of gauge flux.
main_result: The authors establish a 'bi-nonsymmorphism' relation, showing that real-space nonsymmorphic symmetries can enforce momentum-space nonsymmorphism via gauge flux, and identifying specific cases where flux requires nonsymmorphism in both spaces.
method: The study uses group cohomology, projective representations of crystallographic groups, and the analysis of finite Heisenberg algebras to derive the correspondence.
model_or_system: The framework applies to crystalline lattices (including moiré systems, spin liquids, and magnetic materials) and artificial crystals (metamaterials) subject to a symmetric gauge flux configuration.
key_observables: The momentum-space 2-cocycle (omega_F), the real-space 2-cocycle (omega), and the flux-induced twist (Lambda_Phi).
important_parameters: The gauge flux form (Phi), the central sublattice (Z), and the arithmetic classes of the space groups.
main_assumptions: The flux components are assumed to be rational, and the flux form is assumed to be G-invariant.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates real-space and momentum-space lattices and their duals; Figure 2 and 3 show the relationship between primitive and central sublattices for different lattice types.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the mathematical framework of projective crystal symmetries, introduces the concept of the central sublattice and flux-induced twist, establishes the bi-nonsymmorphism relation, and concludes with an exhaustive classification and verification across various arithmetic classes.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) The lattice L and the central sublattice Z (filled lattice points) for real-space Pc with the given Φ. (b) The dual lattices LF and ZF in momentum space. LF (filled lat- tice points) is a sublattice of ZF . Pc preserves ZF . The shaded regions in (a) and (b) correspond to the unit cells of Z and ZF , respectively.

FIG. 2. (a) The base-centered lattice L and its central prim- itive sublattice Z (filled points) for Ccc2 under the given Φ. (b) The dual lattices: base-centered LF (filled points) and primitive ZF . Pba2 preserves ZF . The shaded regions indi- cate the unit cells of Z and ZF , respectively.

FIG. 3. (a) The face-centered lattice L and its body-centered central sublattice Z (filled points) for Fdd2 under the given Φ. (b) The dual lattices: body-centered LF (filled points) and face-centered ZF . Fdd2 preserves ZF . The shaded regions indicate the unit cells of Z and ZF , respectively.
